english_query: counterexample to: A theodicy T successfully reconciles the existence of a benevolent omnipotent de
ugf_response:
The first idea in the case is a claim that a certain story, let us call it story T, does a good job of making fit the thought of a kind all powerful god with the fact that bad things happen. The person who makes the claim says that story T shows how the two can sit together without a clash. The second idea is an example that is offered to show that story T does not work. In this example we see a situation where a very bad hurt happens to a person who could not have caused it, and story T tries to say that this hurt is part of a larger plan that makes sense. The person giving the example says that if we look at the hurt and the plan, they do not line up, so the story cannot make the two fit.

From the first idea the speaker thinks that because story T can make the two fit in some cases, it must be able to do so for all cases. This is the step where the speaker moves from the claim about the story to a broader claim about all hurt. The thinking here is that one success means universal success. The third idea is the judgment about whether this step is good. The step is not strong because a single example where the story fails shows that the story cannot be said to work for every case. The example that does not fit shows that the move from one case to all cases is a jump that is not supported. So the thinking that the first claim leads to the broad claim is weak. In short, the first claim may be true in some places, the second idea shows a place where it does not hold, and the reasoning that the first claim then covers everything is not a good one.

english_query: analogy_proper analogy (explanatory) — cooking_and_craft
ugf_response:
The first thought in the talk is that a right way of showing how two things are alike can be used. The speaker says that the way we make food and the way we make things by hand share a lot of the same steps. That claim is a real claim because both activities need a plan, need tools, need a hand, and need a goal. When we look at those parts we can see that the claim that they are alike is true.

The second thought is that because the two are alike, what we learn from one can be used for the other. The speaker says that if we see how we make food we can then understand how we make things by hand, and that we can take the same ideas and use them in the other case. This is the idea that the first claim leads to a new idea about how we can move from one activity to the other.

The third thought is about whether that move is good. The thinking is partly good because it points out real parts that are the same, but it can also be a little weak if it forgets the parts that are not the same. The step from the first claim to the second claim works well for the parts that are shared, but it may not work as well for the parts that are different. So the overall thinking is useful, but it needs to keep an eye on the parts that do not match.

english_query: how to pick the best reason out of many that could be right
ugf_response: First, we see that there are many possible choices for why something happened. Then we look at the facts we have about the thing that happened. Next, for each possible choice we ask how well it matches those facts. If a choice matches most of the facts, we think it is a good match; if it matches only a few, we think it is a weaker match. After we have looked at all the matches, we compare them and see which one matches the most facts. We also think about how simple the choice is, because a simple match that fits the facts is often better than a very complicated one. Finally, we pick the choice that matches the most facts and is simple enough, and we call that the best reason.

english_query: analogy_proper analogy (argumentative) — precedent
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person says is true is that one thing that happened before is a real picture of how another thing is a lot like it. They point out that the two things share a lot of the same parts and that this sharing is not just a guess but something that can be seen. Because they say the sharing is real, they think we can look at the way the first thing turned out and learn from it.

The second idea they think follows from that is that the way the first thing turned out must tell us how the new thing will turn out. They say that since the first thing ended in a certain way, the new thing should end in the same way because the two are alike in the ways they pointed out. In their view, the past case gives a guide for what will happen now.

When we look at the thinking, we see that it can be good if the parts that are the same are the important parts that decide what happens. If the things that are different are not important for the result, then using the past case can be a helpful guide. But if the differences are about the parts that really matter for how things end, then the thinking is weak because the picture of similarity does not cover what makes the result happen. So the reasoning can be good when the shared parts are the key parts, and it can be bad when the important parts are different. The overall quality of the thinking depends on how well the person checked whether the things that truly decide the result are the same in both cases.

english_query: when helping the group costs you but helps everyone
ugf_response:
Person 1: I see you staying late to finish the work for the team. Why do you do that?  
Person 2: I stay because the group needs the work done and if I do it now it saves time for all.  
Person 1: Does it make you feel tired or take away from your own plans?  
Person 2: Yes, it takes my free time and I feel tired, but I think it is right.  
Person 1: How does it help the rest of the group?  
Person 2: When the work is done, the other people can move on with their tasks and do not have to wait.  
Person 1: So your giving up time makes the whole group work better?  
Person 2: Right, my giving up a little bit lets the whole group move ahead and finish more.  
Person 1: Do you think the group will try to give back or help you later?  
Person 2: I hope they will notice and try to share the work next time.

english_query: counterexample to: A painting represents an object if and only if it visually resembles that object
ugf_response: The first idea says that a painting shows a thing when the picture looks like that thing. The second idea adds that the picture must also look like the thing whenever the painting shows that thing, so the two are said to go together in both directions. The third idea is a case that shows the first idea does not work in both directions: a painting can be made to tell us about a thing even though the picture does not look like the thing, for example a map that uses symbols to stand for a place or a portrait that uses very abstract shapes to stand for a person. This case shows that the claim that a painting shows a thing only when it looks like that thing is not always right. The thinking that follows from the first two ideas is therefore not good, because it takes the first idea as a rule that works for all paintings, but the case shows a clear break in that rule. So the overall argument that the rule works both ways is weak, and the case they give is a good way to point out the problem.

english_query: anomaly analogy (predictive) — geometric
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person is saying is that there is an odd thing that shows up in a picture of shape. They point out that this odd thing can be seen and that it does not fit the normal pattern of the picture. In that sense the claim that the odd thing is there and can be seen is true.

The second idea that follows from that claim is that the odd thing looks a lot like an odd thing that has been seen in a different picture. Because the two odd things look alike, the person says we can compare them and then guess that the rule that made the first odd thing happen will also make the second odd thing happen. In other words, they think the first odd thing can be used as a guide to guess what will happen with the second.

The third idea is an evaluation of the thinking. The line of thought works if the two odd things are truly made by the same hidden rule, but it can be weak if the two pictures are only similar on the outside. The reasoning is okay when there is other proof that the same hidden rule is at work, but it is not very strong when the only link is that the two odd things look alike. So the thinking can be useful as a first guess, but it should not be taken as a firm proof without more checking.

english_query: indicative conditional, modus_ponens — weather_and_planning
ugf_response: First, the person says that if it rains then the plan will change and also says that it is raining right now. This is the first idea. The second idea is that the plan will change because we have a rule that says if rain happens then the plan changes and we see rain happening. This step follows from the two statements. The way we go from the two statements to the third one is a simple step that many people use. It works because we have a rule that joins the first thing to the second thing, and we have seen the first thing, so we can say the second thing. This shows that the thinking is good. It does not add anything that is not already in the two statements, so it is a safe step.

english_query: counterexample to: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point
ugf_response:
The first idea they tell us is that they have a rule that takes any number and gives another number, and that at a certain place this rule makes the answer jump from one value to another. They say that this really does happen, and that part is true – the rule they wrote does make a sudden change at that place.  

The second idea they think follows is that because this one rule can jump at a place, the statement that every rule that takes numbers to numbers does not jump at a place must be wrong. They are using the fact that one case that shows the first idea can be wrong is enough to say the first idea does not hold for all rules.  

The thinking here is good. When a claim says something is true for all rules, finding just one rule that does not behave that way is enough to show the claim is not always true. Since the rule they gave does fit the description of a rule that takes numbers to numbers, the step from the first idea to the second idea follows correctly.

english_query: why some deals hold together and others fall apart
ugf_response: First, when two or more people say they will do something together they make a promise. Then each person must keep what they said and must feel that the other people also keep theirs. If everyone keeps the promise the promise stays together and the people can keep working. If one person does not keep what they said the other people may stop keeping theirs too and the promise can break. The chance that a promise stays together gets stronger when the people talk a lot, when they understand what each other needs, when they feel safe that the other will not hurt them, and when the promise fits what each can do. When the promise is too hard, when people do not talk, when one person changes what they can do, or when someone feels they are not being treated fair, the promise can fall apart. So trust, clear talk, fair treatment and the ability to do what was promised keep a promise together, while broken trust, no talk, feeling hurt and too much change make a promise fall apart.

english_query: how the way choices are shown can change what you pick
ugf_response: First, we notice that when we are asked to pick something we often see a few things at a time. Then we see that the order of those things can make us look at the first one more. This means that the first thing gets more attention because our eyes go there first. Next, we think that if the first thing looks easy or nice we may think it is the best. This can push us to pick it even if another thing might be better. Then we see that the way the things are put together can make us think they belong together, so we may pick a thing from the same group as the first. This means the way the choices are shown can change what we end up picking.

english_query: indicative conditional, defeasible — procedural
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person tells us is a claim that says if something is true then something else should happen. They say the part about the first thing being true is a real fact, and they also say the part about the second thing being a good step follows from the first part. In other words, they are saying that a certain action that is already happening gives us a reason to do another action.

The second idea is the step that they think must come after the first claim. They think that because the first part is true, we have to act in the way they describe. They treat the if‑then claim as a guide for what we should do next, and they say that this guide works for us when we see the first part happen.

The third idea is a check on whether that step makes sense. The claim they make can be changed if other facts appear that point in a different direction. Because the guide can be set aside when new information shows a better way, the thinking is not always solid. It works in some cases, but it is not a rule that can never be broken, so the reasoning is only partly good.

english_query: counterexample to: An inference from particular instances to a general conclusion is inductively st
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person is telling us is that there is a case where a guess that moves from one small thing to a big idea about many things does not work well. In that case a person looks at a few small things, makes a guess that all things like those will be the same, and then finds that the guess is wrong because the later things are not the same. The speaker says this case is true and can be seen as a real happening, not just a thought.

The second idea that follows from that first idea is that, because this one case shows a mistake, the whole belief that a guess from a few small things to a big idea about many things is always a good guess must be false. The speaker thinks that one bad guess is enough to say the belief does not hold in any situation.

The third part is to look at whether that thinking is good. It is true that one bad guess shows that a guess can be weak, but it does not automatically mean that the belief never works. A single case can point out a limit, yet the belief might still be useful in many other situations. To decide if the belief is overall good, we would need to look at many more cases and see how often the guess works. So the thinking that one case completely breaks the belief is not fully strong; it shows a problem but does not by itself prove the belief is always wrong.

english_query: the problem of hurting one person to help many
ugf_response: First, we see that a person can do something that makes another person feel hurt. Then we see that the same act can also make a lot of other people feel better or get something good. This makes us think about what is the right thing to do. We can look at two parts: the part where one person feels hurt, and the part where many people get good. We can think that the hurt to one is a bad thing, but the good to many is a good thing. When we put these two parts together, we have to decide if the good to many is big enough to make the bad to one okay. Some people say that we should not let anyone feel hurt, even if many get good, because the hurt is not right. Others say that if the good is very big, it may be okay to let the one feel hurt. This makes the choice hard. In the end, we see that the idea is about looking at a bad thing that hits one against a good thing that hits many, and there is no easy answer that works for every case.

english_query: indicative conditional, paradoxical — perceptual
ugf_response:
The first idea says that a normal if then line that talks about what we see can be a puzzling thing. It is true that we can have a line like “if I see a red light then I stop” and still feel a clash when the light seems to be both red and green at the same time in a trick. The claim that such a line can feel puzzling is therefore a real point that matches what people can notice.

The second idea says that because the first point can be puzzling, we must change how we think about what we see or must change the line itself. This thought follows from the first point in the sense that the puzzling feeling makes us want to fix the line. However, the step from a puzzling feeling to a need to change all of our seeing habits is not a strong step. Just because one line can be confusing does not mean that every line about seeing must be changed, so the link between the first point and the change is weak.

The third idea looks at the whole thinking. The first point is correct and matches real experience. The jump to a big change in how we think about seeing is not well supported, so the overall thinking is not very good. The argument has a good start but the move from a puzzling line to a big change is a weak move, making the whole case only partly sound.

english_query: literal_similarity analogy (explanatory) — analytical
ugf_response:
The first idea in the story is that two things look the same in a clear way. The person who makes the claim says that the first thing has a shape or a pattern that matches the second thing, and that this matching part is true when we look at them both. The second idea is that because the two things match, the first thing can be used to tell us why the second thing works or why it is right. In other words, the speaker thinks that the matching part lets us move from the first thing to a claim about the second thing, and that this move is a good way to give an answer or a reason. 

When we look at the thinking, we see that the step from the first idea to the second idea depends on more than just the matching part. Just because two things share a look does not always mean that the reasons behind one are the same as the reasons behind the other. The thinking would be stronger if there were more proof that the inside parts or the causes of the two things are also the same, not only the outside look. So the move from the first idea to the second idea can work in some cases, but it is not always a safe way to get a true answer. The reasoning is okay when the match is deep, but it can be weak when the match is only on the surface.

english_query: counterexample to: A society S has made genuine historical progress over a period P if and only if
ugf_response:
The first point they say is true is that a group of people has gotten better in the past during a time when a certain thing is true about that group and that time. They point to the past and say that when the thing is there, the group moves forward and shows signs of getting better.  

From that they think a second point follows: that the certain thing must always be there for any group to get better, and that if a group does not have that thing, then it cannot have gotten better. In other words they say the thing is both needed and enough for improvement, so they treat it as the only rule for getting better.  

When we look at a third point we see that the step from the first point to the second point is not always right. There are many ways a group can get better without the certain thing, and there are examples that show groups improving while lacking it. Because of that the thinking that the thing is required for all improvement is not fully right, even though the first point may be true in some cases.

english_query: why what you already believe changes how you see new things
ugf_response: First, we have a lot of thoughts that we have before in our head. When we look at a new thing our eyes bring the thing to our head and our head uses the thoughts we have before. This makes the new thing look like the old thoughts. If the old thoughts say the thing is good we are more likely to see the new thing as good. If the old thoughts say the thing is bad we may see the new thing as bad. Our head does this because it wants to keep things simple and not have to start each time. So the thoughts we have before act as a way that lets some things of the new thing in and stops other things. This can make us see some things and not see other things. As a result the same new thing can look different to two people who have different thoughts before in their heads. In short what we have before in our head makes the way we look at and know new things change.

english_query: analogy_proper analogy (heuristic) — algebraic
ugf_response: The first idea says that using a way of comparing one thing to another can act like a simple guess way. It is true that a quick compare can give a fast way to think about something new, because we can use what we know about the first thing to guess about the second. The second idea says that because this compare works in a place that uses signs for numbers and letters, it will also work in other places. That jump does not always follow, because the rules that make the first place work may not be the same in other places. The thinking is good when the two places share a similar shape, but it is not good when they are quite different, so the overall reasoning is only partly useful.

english_query: what makes someone happy in the deepest way, not just for a moment
ugf_response: First, we notice that a quick fun thing can make a person feel good for a short time. Then we see that a feeling that stays for a long time needs something that does not end when the quick fun thing ends. This means the person needs a sense that they are part of something that keeps on giving, such as love from family, care from friends, a chance to grow and learn, or a chance to help other people. Next, we think that when a person can see that what they do matters to other people, they feel a strong good feeling that stays. This shows that the feeling comes from being safe, being loved, being able to use the brain and the body to do things that matter, and from having hope that things will keep going well. Finally, we understand that the most lasting good feeling is built from many small good things that stay together over time, not just one quick fun event.

english_query: how to think about what is fair when people start in different places
ugf_response: First, we notice that people can start at not the same spot in life. Then we see that if we want to make a right choice about how to treat them, we have to think about what each one needs and what each one can do. This means we look at the chance each one has to get what they need. Next, we think that if we give the same amount to everyone, the ones who start lower may still end up with less because they had less to begin with. This shows that we may need to give more to the ones who start lower so that the chance for each one to reach a good spot becomes more the same. Then we see that this idea can be used by looking at each person’s start place and giving them what they need to have a similar chance to move forward. This means that a right way to think about what is good for all when people start in different places is to try to make the chance for each one to do well more even, even if that means not giving the exact same thing to each.

english_query: counterexample to: A narrative N constitutes a legitimate historical narrative if and only if (i) N
ugf_response:
The first idea in the case is that the story they call N has a certain property, which they say is true. They point out that the story includes a part that matches what we can see or hear from the past, and they say that this match is a fact that we can check. In other words, they claim that the story really does have the part (i) that they say it has, and they treat that as a true statement about the story.

The second idea is what they think follows from that fact. From the claim that the story has part (i), they say that the whole story must count as a proper real story about the past. They move from the single true part to the whole claim that the story is a good story that can be used to tell what really happened. In short, they think that because the story has that one true part, it must be a full and correct story about the past.

The thinking that links the first idea to the second idea can be checked for how good it is. The step from having one true part to saying the whole story is correct is not always a safe step. It is possible for a story to have a piece that matches the past while still having other pieces that are made up or wrong. Because of that, the move from the first idea to the second idea is a weak one. The reasoning would be stronger if they also showed that all other parts of the story match the past, or if they explained why having that one true part is enough to make the whole story correct. As it stands, the thought that the whole story is good just because of that one true part is not a good piece of thinking.

english_query: how paying close attention to what someone needs is a kind of good
ugf_response: First, we see that when we watch very hard at what a person wants we can find out if that person has a problem or a want that we can help with. Then we notice that when we give that person what they want the person feels safe and happy. This feeling is a kind of good thing because it makes the world a little more nice. Next, we think that when we do this many times more people will want to do the same and the whole group will feel more good. This shows that watching carefully what a person wants and then giving it is a way to make good happen. Finally, we understand that this kind of good does not need big things, just the doing of seeing and helping with the small things that a person wants.

english_query: indicative conditional, modus_tollens — experimental
ugf_response:
The first point the person makes is that there is a rule about statements that say if one thing is true then another thing is true. They say that this rule can be used when we look at a test that shows whether the second thing is true or not. The second point they make is that when the test shows the second thing is not true, the rule lets us say that the first thing must also not be true. The third point they add is that this way of moving from the test result to the claim about the first thing is a normal way to think in a test setting. 

The next part of the case is what they think follows from those points. They think that because the test showed the second thing did not happen, we can be sure that the first thing did not happen either. They also think that this gives us a firm answer about the first thing without needing to look at it directly. 

The final part is an evaluation of that thinking. The way of moving from the test result to the claim about the first thing is a good way to think if we already know that the rule “if first then second” is true in every case. If that rule is only sometimes true, then the step may not be safe and the claim about the first thing could be wrong. Also, a test may not show every detail that could affect the rule, so we should be careful and check that the rule really does hold before we trust the conclusion. In short, the reasoning can be sound, but it depends on the truth of the original rule and on the test covering the right situation.

english_query: what it means for a case to be put together well
ugf_response: First, we see that a case is a group of facts or ideas that are placed in a shape so a person can see what is happening. Then we notice that when the parts are put in a clear order, when the words fit the ideas, and when the important parts are not hidden, the case is put together well. This means that the person reading can follow the line of thought, can know why each part matters, and can see the point that the writer wants to make. A case that is put together well will have a start that tells what is being looked at, a middle that shows the facts and how they link, and an end that tells what we can learn or do. It will not jump around or leave out key parts, and it will use words that match the ideas. When all of this happens, the reader can understand the case without needing extra help.

english_query: probabilistic conditional, modus_ponens — child_rearing
ugf_response:
The first idea in the case is a claim about what is true. It says that when a parent talks a lot to a child, the child is more likely to start talking a lot too. The claim also says that we can see the parent doing the talking a lot, so we have a real example of the first part of the claim. In other words the case says we have a situation where the first thing happens and we think the second thing should happen because the first thing usually leads to the second.

The second idea is what the case thinks follows from that claim. It uses the way we think about “if one thing happens then another thing will happen” and the fact that we have seen the first thing. From those two pieces it says we can say that the child will start talking a lot. This is the step that takes the claim about a link between parent talking and child talking and the observation of the parent talking, and turns it into a new statement about the child.

The third idea looks at whether that step is a good step. The step is good if the link between the two things is strong enough that seeing the first thing really makes the second thing very likely. If the link is only a weak guess, then moving from the first thing to the second thing can be shaky. In this case the step works well when there is good evidence that children copy the amount of talking they hear from parents. If that evidence is missing or only a little, then the thinking is not as strong and we should be careful before saying the child will definitely talk a lot. So the reasoning can be good when the link is strong, but it can be weak when the link is only a weak guess.

english_query: probabilistic conditional, modus_tollens — perceptual
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person says is that there is a link between a chance event and a sign we can see. In other words they say that if something is likely to happen, then we would expect to see a sign that shows it is on its way. The person also says that we do not see that sign. The second idea that the person puts forward is that because the sign is not seen, the first thing – the chance event – must not be likely or must not be true. They are using a rule that works like this: if A would make B happen and B is not happening, then A is not happening. The way they think about it is that the rule can be used even when A is only probably true, not only when A is for sure true.

The way the thinking goes is that the first idea is taken as a fact, the second idea is taken as a result, and then the person says that the result shows the first idea is not likely. This step works well when the link between A and B is a sure one – when we know that A always makes B happen, then not seeing B does tell us that A did not happen. But when the link is only a matter of chance, the step is not as strong. Not seeing the sign does not prove that the chance event did not happen; it only makes it a little less likely. So the thinking mixes a rule that works for sure cases with a case that is only about chance.

Because of this mix, the thinking is not fully good. The first idea may be true, and the rule they use is a good rule for sure cases, but using it for a chance case makes the conclusion weaker than they claim. A better way to think would be to say that not seeing the sign makes the chance event less likely, not that it is definitely not true. This keeps the thinking in line with the kind of link they first talked about.

english_query: why sharing all you know is not always the best move
ugf_response: First, we think about why you might want to tell all the facts you have. Then we see that sometimes when you tell all the facts, other people may feel bad or may not want to act, and this can make them stop listening. Also, sometimes keeping some facts for later can help you keep power or keep a plan safe. Also, if you share everything at once, the other people may not have time to think about each part and they may miss the important parts. So it can be better to choose what to share and when. This is why sharing all you know is not always the best move.

english_query: counterexample to: An omission O by a person P is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) P
ugf_response:
The first idea says that when a person does not do something, that not doing can be the law reason for a hurt only when certain conditions are met. Looking at what we know, it is true that a not doing can sometimes be the law reason for a hurt, but it is not true that this happens in every situation. So the first idea is partly right and partly not right.  

The second idea says that because there is a case where the not doing meets those conditions, that means the idea works for all other situations. In other words, they think that one case shows the whole idea is always correct.  

The thinking in the second idea is not good because a single case does not prove that the idea works for every case. The move from one case to all cases skips the need to check many other times, and that makes the reasoning weak. It would be better to look at many different cases before saying the idea is always true.

english_query: counterexample to: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed in
ugf_response:
The first idea that is being said is that there is a rule that says a thing is a simple thing that cannot be be broken into smaller parts exactly when it cannot be be broken into smaller parts. The case that is given is a thing that we will call X. The claim about X is that X cannot be be broken into smaller parts, but X is not a simple thing because it is still made of something that we cannot see. The statement that X cannot be be broken into smaller parts is true, and the statement that X is not a simple thing is also said to be true.

From that the second idea that is thought to follow is that the rule is not correct. The thought is that because we have found a thing that has the first condition but does not have the second condition, the rule cannot work for every thing. So the thought is that the rule must be changed or stopped.

The third idea looks at whether that thought is good. The step from the case to the claim that the rule is wrong is correct if the rule was meant to work both ways, that is, if it said “if and only if”. If the rule only said “if”, then the case does not break it. Also the thought is only good if X really is not a simple thing. If X really is a simple thing, then the case does not show a problem and the thinking is not good. If X truly is not a simple thing, then the thinking that the rule is wrong is a good move.

english_query: counterexample to: A sentence presupposes a proposition if and only if (i) the truth of the proposi
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person says is that a sentence takes for granted a certain statement when two things happen together. The first of those two things is that if the statement is true then the whole sentence also comes out true. The second of those two things is that if the statement is true then the opposite of the whole sentence comes out false. They hold this up as the way a sentence shows that it takes for granted something.

From that they think the second idea follows: if we can find a sentence where those two things do not both happen, then the rule they gave cannot be right. They point to a case where a sentence seems to take for granted a statement, but the truth of that statement does not make the whole sentence true, or the truth of that statement does not make the opposite of the sentence false. Because that case shows the two things are not both there, they say the rule is broken.

When we look at whether the thinking is good, we see that the first idea describes a pattern that works for many sentences, but it does not cover every way a sentence can take for granted something. The second idea correctly shows a case that does not fit the pattern, so the claim that the pattern is always true is not well supported. The overall thinking is strong because it uses a clear case to show that the rule needs to be changed or limited, even though the first idea was presented as a universal rule.

english_query: material conditional, modus_ponens — deontological
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person says is that there is a kind of statement that works like this: it says that when one thing is true, another thing will also be true. The person also says that there is a simple rule for using that kind of statement. The rule works like this: if we know a statement that says if one thing is true then another thing is true, and we also know that the first thing really is true, then we can say that the second thing must be true as well. The person adds a third idea that this way of thinking fits with a view that says we must follow rules about what we should do.

The second idea that follows from the first two is that because the rule lets us move from the first part of the statement to the second part, we can decide what we ought to do by looking at the rule and the first part. In other words, if the rule says that doing X means we should do Y, and we see that X is happening, then we can say that we should do Y. The person thinks that this shows that the rule about how we move from one part to the next supports the view that we must follow rules about what we should do.

The third idea looks at whether the thinking is good. The step from the first part of the statement to the second part follows the rule correctly, so that part of the thinking is sound. The jump from the rule to the view about what we should do, however, adds an extra belief that the rule about statements also tells us how we must act. That extra belief is not forced by the rule itself; it adds a belief about what we ought to do. Because the extra belief is not proved by the earlier steps, the overall thinking is only partly good. The part that uses the rule to get from the first thing to the second thing is solid, but the part that says this also tells us how we must act goes beyond what the rule alone can give.

english_query: material conditional, modus_ponens — consequentialist
ugf_response:
The first idea they put forward is a simple rule about “if … then …”. It says that a statement that says one thing will happen if another thing is true is only wrong when the first thing is true and the second thing is not true. In all other cases the rule is taken as true. This is a basic description of how the “if … then …” rule works in everyday talk.

The second idea they take from that rule is a step that most people use when they have both parts of the rule. They say that because we have the “if … then …” rule and we also know that the first part of it is true, we can then say that the second part must also be true. In other words, if we accept the rule and we see the first condition happening, we can safely say the result happens. This step follows the usual pattern that people use when they move from a condition to its result.

The third idea they try to build on the first two is a claim about what makes an action right. They say that because we can link a condition to a result, the goodness of an action is decided by the result it brings. In short, they argue that the best outcome makes the action the right one to do. This claim moves from a rule about how statements work to a claim about how we should judge actions.

Looking at the three ideas together, the first idea is a correct description of the “if … then …” rule, so it can be taken as true. The second idea follows correctly from the first when the first condition is indeed true, so the thinking in that step is good. However, the jump from those two ideas to the third idea is not forced by the rule about statements. The rule tells us how we can move from a true condition to a true result, but it does not tell us that the value of an action is decided by the result alone. Therefore the thinking that leads to the third idea is not well supported by the first two ideas, even though the first two parts are sound.

english_query: what makes a good reason for believing something
ugf_response:
Person 1: What makes it okay for us to think something is true?  
Person 2: We look for things we can see or hear that match the idea, and that other people also see or hear.  
Person 1: How do we know those things can be trusted?  
Person 2: We check if they happen the same way many times and if they fit with other things we already know.  
Person 1: What if two different things both seem to match the idea?  
Person 2: Then we compare which one fits better with the most other things we know and which one has been seen by more people.  
Person 1: Can a feeling be a good cause for thinking something is true?  
Person 2: A feeling can help, but we need something we can see or hear too, because feelings can change a lot.  
Person 1: So the best cause for thinking something is true is a mix of what we see, what many people see, and how it fits with other things we know.  
Person 2: Yes, that mix gives us a strong way to think it is true.

english_query: why what happens after you act matters for whether it was right
ugf_response:
The first idea says that the thing that comes after you do something is part of what makes the act feel right or not right. It tells us that we cannot look at the act alone, we must also look at what happens later, because that later part can change how we think about the whole thing.

The second idea says that because the later part matters, we should use what comes after to decide if the first act was good or not good. In other words, we should watch the whole chain of events and let the ending tell us whether the start was right.

The thinking behind these ideas can be good in some ways and not so good in other ways. It is good because it makes us think about the whole picture and not just a single moment, and that can help us avoid saying a thing is right when it later brings harm or trouble. It is not so good because sometimes the later part is not under the control of the person who acted, and it can be caused by other people or by chance. In those cases, judging the first act only by the later part can be unfair. So the thought that the later part matters is useful, but we also need to remember that the later part does not always belong to the first person, and we should keep that in mind when we decide if the first act was right.

english_query: why someone's character does not decide if their point is right
ugf_response:
We see a person speak and give an idea about how something should be or what is true. At the same time we also see how that person lives, whether they are kind or cruel, honest or dishonest, fair or unfair. The question that pops up is whether the way a person lives should make us think that the idea they share is right or wrong. In other words, does a person’s good or bad way of being decide if the thing they say is true?

The hard part of this question is that it mixes two different kinds of things. One kind is about a person’s habits, choices and how they treat other people. The other kind is about a claim or a fact that can be checked by looking at the world. It is easy to think that a kind person must be right and a cruel person must be wrong, because we like to trust people who act well. But it is also easy to see that a kind person can still be mistaken, and a cruel person can still say something that matches what we see in the world. The question therefore is: should we judge the idea by the person, or should we judge the idea by its own match to reality?

One answer that many people give is “yes, the person matters.” They say that if a person is known to lie or cheat, we should be careful about believing what they say, because they have shown they do not care about the truth. They also say that a person who has acted with care and honesty is more likely to give a true idea, so we can give them more trust. This answer comes from the feeling that we need a quick way to decide whom to listen to, and the past behavior of a person is an easy shortcut.

Another answer that many people give is “no, the person does not decide.” They argue that a claim can be checked by looking at facts, experiments, or other people’s reports, and that the truth of the claim does not change because the speaker is kind or cruel. They point out that history is full of good people who were wrong about many things, and of bad people who accidentally said something that turned out to be right. For them the right way is to test the claim itself, not the speaker.

A third view tries to hold both sides together. It says that while the person’s way of being should not be the only thing we use, it is still useful as a hint. If a person has a long record of being honest, we might give their claim a little more weight while we still check the claim. If a person has a long record of lying, we should be extra careful and look harder for proof before we accept the claim. This view tries to keep the focus on the claim but also uses the person’s past as a guide.

All of these answers come from the same place: we want to know when we can trust what someone says. The debate shows that the way a person acts and the truth of what they say are two separate tracks, and we have to decide how much the first track should pull on the second.

english_query: counterexample to: A person P trusts a person Q if and only if (i) P and Q are personally acquainte
ugf_response:
The case first says that there is a situation where one person, let us call the first person P, feels safe with another person Q even though P has never met Q before and does not know Q. In the usual claim, the idea is that P feels safe with Q only when P and Q know each other. The case points out that this example does not fit that idea because the two people do not know each other yet P still feels safe. This part of the case is true: the example does show a time when the usual idea does not hold.

From that true part, the person who gave the case thinks that the usual idea must be wrong. They say that because we have at least one example where P feels safe with Q without knowing Q, the whole statement that feeling safe only happens when people know each other cannot be right. This is the second idea in the case.

The thinking that follows from the true example is good. When a claim says that something happens in all cases, finding even a single case where it does not happen is enough to show the claim is not true in general. So the move from the example to saying the original claim is false is a sound move. It does not prove what always makes people feel safe, only that personal knowing is not a required condition, and the reasoning respects the way a universal claim can be refuted.

english_query: how to think about what the other person will do before you act
ugf_response:
Person 1: I often think about how I can think about what you will do before I do something.  
Person 2: You can start by looking at what you know I like and what I have done before.  
Person 1: So I should look at the things I have seen you do in the past?  
Person 2: Yes, that helps you guess what I might do now. You can also ask me what I think about the choice.  
Person 1: But what if I cannot ask you first?  
Person 2: Then you can think about what you have seen me pick in like times. You can also think about what you want to happen and try to fit that with what I might do.  
Person 1: How do I know if my guess is right?  
Person 2: After you do your thing, you can see if what I did fits your guess. If it does, you learn that your way works. If it does not, you can change how you guess next time.

english_query: literal_similarity analogy (heuristic) — metaphysics
ugf_response:
The first thing they say is that looking at how one thing is like another in a simple way can be used as a way to think about hard thoughts about what is true in the world. They point out that when two things look the same in a plain, literal way, that similarity can be taken as a clue that they may share something deeper about what is true. This part of the claim can be true in some small examples where the plain look really does match a deeper match.

The second thing they think follows from that is that because the plain look works as a clue in a few small times, it can be used as a rule for many other times when we want to know what is true about the world. They say that if a plain look tells us something in one case, then we can use the same plain look to decide what is true in many other cases, even when we have not looked at those other things directly.

The third thing is an assessment of whether that line of thought is good. It is true that a plain look can sometimes give a useful clue, but it is not a strong way to decide what is true in many different situations. Things that look the same on the outside can be very different on the inside, and things that look different on the outside can share the same deep truth. So using the plain look as a rule for all other times is a weak step, and the thinking does not hold up well when we try to apply it to many hard questions about what is true.

english_query: counterexample to: A division of history into periods is legitimate if and only if (i) each period
ugf_response:
The first idea says that a way of cutting the past into time parts is right when each time part has a clear start and a clear end and when each part has its own main thing happening. The claim also says that when those two things are true for every part, then the whole cutting of the past is a good way to look at the past.

The second idea gives a case that seems to go against the claim. In that case a person looks at a long stretch of the past and makes three time parts. Each of those three parts does have a clear start, a clear end, and its own main thing happening, so the first condition of the claim is met. Yet the three parts together still leave out an important link between the first and the second part, and the way the three parts are put together does not help a person see how the whole past works. Because of that, the person says the cutting is not a good way to look at the past even though the two conditions of the claim were met.

The thinking that the case shows the claim is wrong depends on whether the case really meets the two conditions. If the case truly has a clear start, a clear end, and its own main thing for each part, then the case does show that those two things are not enough to make the cutting a good way to look at the past, and the thinking is strong. If, however, the case actually fails to keep one of the two things clear – for example if the start or end is fuzzy or the main thing is not really separate – then the case does not break the claim and the thinking that it does is weak. So the quality of the thinking rests on how well the case follows the two conditions that the first idea set out.
